entry.title: Re: Alternative ways to automatically determine Storage L...
entry.content:
Dear @Harukiy,

There is no second standard configuration mechanism comparable to Assign Picking Locations for automatically determining the standard Storage Location field in the Sales Order or Outbound Delivery. For the Sales Order, SAP confirms that automatic storage location determination is not part of the standard Public Cloud design. SAP explicitly positions this as a feature request, not a software defect

More details below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003565924

Therefore, if the requirement is to determine the SLoc already at Sales Order item level the realistic options are not additional SD configuration settings but design alternatives:

1. Use aATP Alternative-Based Confirmation only if the requirement is availability-driven

If the business requirement is use another storage location when stock is available there then Alternative-Based Confirmation can be considered. SAP documents that ABC can replace the originally requested delivering plant, storage location or product for a sales order requirement

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/98482586b3b949579ebcb58cfb7c932d.html

This should not be positioned as general SLoc defaulting. It is an aATP substitution process, mainly relevant where the storage location decision is driven by ATP confirmation logic. SAP also lists important restrictions for example that ABC supports MTS scenarios and specific sales order/item category conditions

More details below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003381688

2. Use an external / side-by-side determination process when the rule is customer-specific

If the storage location must be derived from customer-specific rules that cannot be represented in standard configuration the clean-core option is to determine the SLoc outside the core logic for example in an order-entry layer, middleware, BTP application or controlled automation and pass it as an explicit value during order or delivery creation. This is not SAP standard determination. It is a deliberate business override. The consequence must be clear if a storage location already exists in the predecessor sales document, SAP copies it into the outbound delivery and does not re-run the delivery storage-location determination

More details below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0002821149

3. BAdI/custom logic as the main solution

For delivery processing, SAP provides shipping-related BAdIs such as LE_SHP_MODIFY_ITEM and LE_SHP_SAVE_DOCUMENT_PREPARE but I would not position them as the preferred SLoc determination approach without validating the exact field availability and creation scenario in the customer tenant. SAP’s own KBAs show that delivery BAdIs have scenario-specific restrictions and not all delivery fields can be safely changed in every creation flow

More details below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003648672

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003723362

Summary:

Apart from Assign Picking Locations the only SAP-standard functional alternative is aATP Alternative-Based Confirmation but only for availability-based substitution scenarios. For customer-specific SLoc rules the clean-core option is an external/side-by-side determination with explicit value entry. There is no additional standard Public Cloud configuration that automatically determines SLoc in the Sales Order item

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Quality Lot Status Not Changed
entry.content: First, reconcile the serial number stock type (move them out of quality if they are consumed). Then, re‑process the usage decision (QA12) to refresh the lot status. If the system still refuses, you will need to perform a technical reversal/re‑posting of the affected serial numbers, because SAP does not allow manual status change for inspection lots. This is a standard restriction: you cannot directly “force change” the lot status. It must be cleared by correcting the underlying serial number postings.

entry.title: Re: Item is blocked for payment
entry.content:
Even though you do not see a block in the document header or master data, the block “C” is most likely set at line item level (BSEG-ZLSPR) or triggered by payment method/configuration checks in FBZP.

So the recommended steps are

  • Run FBL5N (customer line items) or FBL1N (vendor line items) with field Payment block displayed, to see if the block is at item level.
  • Check FBZP settings for payment methods and company code.
  • If the block is incorrect, you can reset the payment block in the line item via FB02 (change document → Payment block field).
  • If it is due to configuration, adjust the payment method settings so the item qualifies for payment.

entry.title: Custom Analytical Query exposed as OData V2 not available in Custom Communication Scenario
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

I am working in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602 with the new app Custom Analytical Queries and Services.

I created a Custom Analytical Query and enabled the option:

Expose as OData V2

The query is successfully published and the activation log shows no errors.

 

Query technical name:

YY1_IMMV2_ONETOLL_EAC

However, when I try to create a Custom Communication Scenario, I cannot find this query/service under Add Services.

I also checked Display Inbound Services, but I cannot find any inbound service related to this query.

My question is:

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602, should a Custom Analytical Query exposed as OData V2 appear as an inbound service that can be added to a Custom Communication Scenario?

What I have already checked:

The query is published.
The flag Expose as OData V2 is enabled.
The activation log is successful.
The service does not appear in Custom Communication Scenarios > Add Services.
The service does not appear in Display Inbound Services.

Any guidance or official SAP documentation for the 2602 release would be appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: Pacemaker workcenter requirement for capacity scheduling board
entry.content:
In SAP Notes such as 3557362 - Issue with "Realign Schedules" and "Schedule Production" Buttons in Capacity Scheduling Board - SAP for Me, a pacemaker workcenter is listed as a requirement for orders to be scheduled via the Capacity Scheduling Board app. As I understand the pacemaker workcenter is expected to be updated in the 'Production Line' field for the material's production version. 

The current client I work for has a production set up where a product can be produced on x similar production lines. The capacity scheduling process is used to optimize the load because the production lines. In such case there is no single pacemaker workcenter per se - does it mean x number of production versions need to be created just to have each of the possible production lines as pacemakers? This creates unnecessary data maintenance requirement in my opinion and make no sense, or have I understood this wrongly?

Appreciate any advice that experts here can provide. Thank you!

Side note: Description for the 'Production Line' field indicates it is for repetitive manufacturing but I suppose it is also relevant for discrete manufacturing now. Perhaps the description needs to be updated...

entry.title: Cash Flow Statement: Reporting Rules vs. Semantic Tags
entry.content:
Overview: Two Approaches to the Same Problem

SAP S/4HANA Cloud offers two methods for generating a Cash Flow Statement using the Indirect Method. The first — the Semantic Tags approach — has been available since early S/4HANA Cloud releases and relies on assigning predefined tags to Financial Statement Version (FSV) nodes. The second — the Reporting Rules approach — is the strategic replacement, introduced to address the limitations of the tag-based model and to align with the requirements of IFRS 18.

Both approaches ultimately display a cash flow statement, but they differ fundamentally in how they select, structure, and maintain the underlying data. This document details those differences dimension by dimension.

 

Process Architecture: Side by Side

Legacy: Semantic Tags

New: Reporting Rules

1.     Define Financial Statement Version (FSV)

2.     Assign Semantic Tags to FSV nodes / G/L accounts

3.     Cash Flow Statement — Indirect Method

4.     Custom Analytical Queries (optional, for customization)

1.     Manage Reporting Rules (F9259) — define reporting logic

2.     Manage Global Hierarchies (F2918) — build hierarchy

3.     Financial Statements Review Booklet (F8587) — display & analyze

Detailed Feature Comparison

The table below compares both approaches across 12 key dimensions. Green cells indicate an advantage held by the Reporting Rules approach.

 

Dimension

Semantic Tags (Legacy)

Reporting Rules (New)

Configuration Approach

Tag-based: assign semantic tags to FSV hierarchy nodes or individual G/L accounts. Tags are predefined by SAP.

Rule-based: define granular selection criteria (account range, debit/credit, transaction type, dimensions) per cash flow line item.

Granularity of Data Selection

FSV-node level. All accounts under a tagged node are included — no sub-filtering possible.

Account range + business transaction type + financial transaction type + profit center / segment / business area / cost center. Full control over what is included.

IFRS 18 Readiness

Not supported. SAP explicitly recommends migrating away from Semantic Tags to support IFRS 18.

Built-in IFRS 18-aligned hierarchy template. Ready for the 2027 effective date with no rework.

Business User Ownership

Requires Key User or developer to restructure FSV and re-assign tags when accounts change.

Finance team can create, edit, and maintain reporting rules independently via Fiori — no IT ticket needed.

Custom Dimension Filtering

Not available in standard. Requires Custom Analytical Queries for any custom logic or dimensions.

Extension Fields natively supported in reporting rules — expose custom journal entry fields as filter criteria.

Reporting Hierarchy Structure

Re-uses the Financial Statement Version (FSV) — same structure as balance sheet/P&L. Cash flow layout is constrained.

Dedicated cash flow hierarchy, independent of the FSV. Structure is freely defined/owned by customers by matching IFRS 18 categories exactly.

Drill-Down Capability

Cash Flow Statement – Indirect Method app provides tag-level data. Limited drill-through to journals.

Financial Statements Review Booklet (F8587) delivers full drill-down from any item to multiple objects such as Trading Partner, G/L account, Journal Entry etc. 

Display App

Cash Flow Statement – Indirect Method (standalone app).

Financial Statements Review Booklet (F8587) — unified with Balance Sheet, P&L, Trial Balance, and Statement of Changes.

Customization Path

Custom Analytical Queries required for non-standard layouts or custom semantic tags.

Extension Fields in Manage Reporting Rules (F9259) handle most customization without custom queries.

Setup Complexity

Moderate: configure FSV, then assign tags to nodes/accounts — but locked to predefined tag types.

Lower initial setup (rules + hierarchy), and far more flexible and future-proof.

SAP Roadmap Alignment

No further development planned. SAP recommends migration.

Strategic solution — actively developed and aligned with IFRS 18, cloud-first roadmap.

 

Key Advantages of Reporting Rules — In Depth

The following sections explain the most significant advantages in practical terms.

 

1

IFRS 18 Compliance — Built In, Not Bolted On

Why it matters: IFRS 18 takes effect for annual periods beginning on or after 1 January 2027.

•       Reporting Rules ships with an IFRS 18-aligned hierarchy template covering the mandatory categories: Operating, Investing, Financing, Integral Associates & JVs, and the reconciliation to opening/closing cash.

•       The Semantic Tags approach was designed before IFRS 18 and has no built-in template for the new structure. Migration is required regardless of whether the customer changes the reporting surface.

•       With Reporting Rules, organizations can adapt their cash flow classification for IFRS 18 by updating rules in Fiori — no developer involvement, no transport needed.

 

2

Granular, Multiple-Level Selection Criteria

Why it matters: Not every G/L account under an FSV node belongs in the same cash flow category.

•       With Semantic Tags, all accounts under a tagged FSV node are included — there is no way to include only a subset of accounts or filter by debit/credit within that node without restructuring the FSV.

•       Reporting Rules let you define account ranges (From / To), account hierarchy node,  Financial transaction type, and additional dimensions — all within a single rule, without touching the account master data.

•       This is critical for scenarios like separating cash payments to suppliers from non-cash provisions that share the same G/L account structure.

 

3

Finance Team Owns the Configuration

Why it matters: Every IT-dependent change slows down period-end close and increases cost.

•       Semantic Tags are predefined by SAP. Adding new tags or creating custom calculation logic requires Custom Analytical Queries — a developer-level task.

•       Reporting Rules are created and maintained entirely within Fiori app F9259 by a finance user with Key User or GL Accountant role. Adding a new cash flow line item, adjusting an account range, or modifying a sign takes minutes.

•       Extension Fields in Manage Reporting Rules additionally allow custom journal entry fields (e.g., project, fund, grant) to become filter criteria — again without ABAP development.

 

4

Dedicated Cash Flow Hierarchy — Decoupled from FSV

Why it matters: FSV restructuring for balance sheet or P&L purposes should not break the cash flow statement.

•       The Semantic Tags approach attaches to the FSV. If the FSV is restructured — for example, to comply with a new country-specific balance sheet format — the tag assignments may need to be reviewed and corrected for the cash flow statement.

•       The Reporting Rules hierarchy is completely independent of the FSV. It lives in Manage Global Hierarchies (F2918) as a separate object of type Reporting Rules Hierarchy.

•       This structural independence eliminates an entire class of configuration errors during FSV maintenance cycles.

 

5

Unified Reporting Experience in the Financial Statements Review Booklet

Why it matters: Finance teams should not need to switch between multiple apps for different statements.

•       The Semantic Tags approach renders the cash flow in a dedicated standalone app: Cash Flow Statement — Indirect Method.

•       The Reporting Rules approach renders the cash flow inside the Financial Statements Review Booklet (F8587) — the same app used for Balance Sheet, P&L, Trial Balance, and Statement of Changes.

•       Users get a single navigation experience across all financial statements, consistent filter behavior, shared comparison period controls, and unified PDF/Excel export.

 

When Should You Migrate?

If you are currently running the Semantic Tags approach, the question is not whether to migrate — SAP has already stated the recommendation clearly. The question is when and how to sequence the transition.

Migrate immediately if any of the following apply:

  • Your organization is preparing for IFRS 18 adoption (mandatory by 2027 for most groups).
  • You are adding new company codes or restructuring your chart of accounts.
  • Your finance team currently raises IT tickets for cash flow line item changes.
  • You need multiple level filtering that the FSV node structure cannot provide.

 

Migration Path: From Semantic Tags to Reporting Rules

Migrating does not require deleting or restructuring your existing FSV. The two approaches can coexist temporarily during transition. A recommended migration sequence:

1.     Step 1 — Analyze your existing FSV and semantic tag assignments to identify which G/L accounts map to each cash flow category.

2.     Step 2 — Create equivalent reporting rules in Manage Reporting Rules (F9259), replicating each line item with explicit G/L account ranges.

3.     Step 3 — Build the new reporting hierarchy in Manage Global Hierarchies (F2918) using the IFRS 18 template.

4.     Step 4 — Run both statements in parallel for one period to validate figures.

5.     Step 5 — Switch to the Financial Statements Review Booklet (F8587) as the primary reporting surface.

 

 

Summary

The Cash Flow Statement Based on Reporting Rules is not merely an incremental improvement over the Semantic Tags approach. It is a fundamentally different configuration model that gives finance teams direct ownership of cash flow data, removes the dependency on FSV structure, enables parallel ledger reporting, and provides a migration path to IFRS 18 — all without writing a line of code.

For any organization planning to stay on SAP S/4HANA Cloud beyond 2026, migrating from Semantic Tags to Reporting Rules is the recommended and strategically correct path.

 

References

  • Cash Flow Statement Based on Reporting Rules (SAP Help): https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/55e9ee97e6d943cda3543b4fa7876ed3.html
  • Cash Flow Statement Based on Semantic Tags (SAP Help): https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/0b84276181f24d62868d170da9c98ab8.html

entry.title: How to Receive Email Notifications from SAP
entry.content:
Our IT system administrators are receiving a large volume of emails from SAP.
We are currently reviewing the settings to ensure that all of these emails are forwarded to the **NAME** administrators.
Please explain SAP notifications and the settings for notification recipients.

My understanding is that email notifications from SAP can be broadly categorized into the following three types:

① ② ③ I understand that each of these categories delivers notifications as follows:
① High-priority communications regarding the Private Cloud Edition
② Notifications related to each subscribed SAP product.
③ Notifications regarding activated items in SAP for Me.

My questions are as follows:
Q1. Is my understanding of the above correct? Please let me know if there are any other notification settings.
Q2. The IT administrator’s S user has been assigned the Cloud Administrator role.
The NAME administrator’s role is SuperAdministrator.
Are there any cases where email notifications cannot be received due to this difference in roles?


--- Support Assistant ---
Q: Please categorize your issue by choosing the option below that best fits your scenario.
A: SAP for Me new release / performance notifications


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.content:

Szilvia

entry.title: Re: Stop requirement transfer when sales order is in approval
entry.content:
Hi @LinglingQian ,

审批中的订单不会被计算到信用敞口是正常的。因为销售订单在审批中，确认的数量会被设置为0。因为信用敞口的计算会考虑确认数量，所以审批中的订单没有被包含在信用敞口的计算当中。

如果您希望被审批的订单出现在信用敞口中，您可以去修改审批请求原因的配置，不要设置“确认数量为0”。

Best regards,

Yong

entry.title: Re: schema group on vendor master ..?
entry.content:
Schema Groups

  • Purpose: Schema Groups are used to group together Suppliers (Vendors) and Purchasing Organizations that follow the same pricing logic or Calculation Schema.
  • Flexibility: It allows the system to differentiate between simple pricing (e.g., only Price + Discount) and complex pricing (e.g., Price + Taxes + Freight + Surcharges).
  • Schema Group for Suppliers: Defined in SPRO and then assigned to the Supplier Master Record (Purchasing Data tab).
  • Schema Group for Purchasing Org: Defined in SPRO and assigned directly to your Purchasing Organization.
  • The Logic: When creating a Purchase Order, SAP combines the Schema Group of the Purchasing Org and the Supplier to determine which specific Pricing Procedure should be triggered.
  • Assignment Rule: If no Schema Group is assigned to a Supplier (kept blank), the system will automatically use the Standard/Default Calculation Schema mapped for that Purchasing Organization.

I hope this information would help you.

entry.title: Clarification Required: No Email Notification on User Removal from Organization (CDC B2B)
entry.content:
Hi Teams,

SAP CDC B2B is designed to support multi-organization membership out of the box, where users can be associated with multiple organizations, and existing users are automatically linked when invited.

I have recently tested this behavior and observed the following:

  • When a user is removed from an organization, the removal is successfully reflected in the system.
  • However, the user does not receive any email notification informing them about this removal.

As per my understanding, there is no out-of-the-box email trigger configured for this specific action in CDC.

Could you please help clarify the following:

  1. Is there any standard configuration available in SAP CDC to send notifications when a user is removed from an organization?
  2. If not, what is the recommended approach to implement this notification?
    • Should this be handled via webhooks + custom logic?
  3. Are there any best practices for implementing such notification flows in CDC B2B environments?

Our goal is to ensure that users are properly informed whenever their access to an organization is revoked.

Looking forward to your guidance on this.

Thanks & Regards,
Bhabani Mohanty

entry.title: Re: Custom Analytical Query exposed as OData V2 not avail...
entry.content:
Dear @IsmaelMateos,

In Public Cloud 2602 a Custom Analytical Query exposed with Expose as OData V2 is not expected to appear as an inbound service in Custom Communication Scenarios and it is also not expected to be listed in Display Inbound Services

SAP position is that external OData APIs require Communication Scenarios, Communication Arrangements and Communication Users. Analytical queries, including Custom Analytical Queries use analytical/internal access protocols and cannot be accessed by external communication users. Therefore, Communication Scenarios and Communication Arrangements cannot be created for analytical queries. SAP confirms this for Public Cloud all versions

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2911981

The 2602 feature documentation confirms that the new Custom Analytical Queries and Services app supports exposing a query as OData or InA Service but this is an analytical consumption capability, not the same as creating a Communication Management inbound service. The same 2602 documentation separately describes Communication Management functions such as Custom Communication Scenarios, Communication Arrangements and Display Inbound Services

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/doc/7c9e0bbbd1664c2581b2038a1c7ae4b3/2602.500/en-US/FSD_CE2602.pdf

Therefore the fact that YY1_IMMV2_ONETOLL_EAC is published successfully but does not appear under Add Services or Display Inbound Services is standard behavior, not an activation issue

For external API consumption with a communication user the supported Public Cloud approach is to create a Custom CDS View with scenario External API and then add this generated service to a Custom Communication Scenario. SAP guidance for custom communication scenarios refers specifically to adding the External API scenario based custom CDS view as the inbound service

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/7855f3f364974887ad93d8647fbf0ee2.html

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3614521

Summary:

A Custom Analytical Query exposed as OData V2 in Public Cloud 2602 does not become an inbound service for Custom Communication Scenarios. If the requirement is external integration via Communication Arrangement / Communication User, rebuild the required dataset as a Custom CDS View External API where the required fields and logic can be modelled without relying on analytical-query-only semantics

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: DMEE Transaction
entry.content:
Hi Keerthana,

You can handle this directly in the DMEE tree by using a Composite Node with two separate technical nodes underneath it—one for the IBAN and one for the Bank Account.

For the IBAN node, add a condition where FPAYH-ZIBAN is not initial. For the Bank Account node, add a condition where FPAYH-ZIBAN is initial, and map it to FPAYH-ZBNKN. This structure ensures the system correctly evaluates the availability of the IBAN first before falling back to the standard vendor account number under the Other -> Id path.

Are you working with a standard ISO 20022 XML format (like SEPA), or is this a completely customized country-specific format?

entry.title: Re: How to add purchase group in J1IGSUBREP Report
entry.content:
Hi Mahil,

You can add the purchasing group (EKGRP) to the J1IGSUBREP report output by utilizing the standard BAdI J1IG_SUBCONTRACTING_BADI.

Inside this BAdI, look for the method CHANGE_HEADER_DETAILS or CHANGE_ITEM_DETAILS depending on where you have extended the output structure with your append structure. You can write your custom SELECT logic there to fetch EKGRP from the EKKO table using the purchase order number (EBELN) available in the report's internal table data.

Have you already created the append structure for the ALV display structure, or do you need the exact structure name to extend first?

entry.title: Re: How can we Approve the PO in program like we do in My...
entry.content:
Hi @Zhao_HT1,

To approve a Purchase Order programmatically exactly how the My Inbox Fiori app does it, you should interact with the underlying Workflow engine rather than using legacy MM function modules.

The most reliable, "Clean Core" method is to use the class CL_SWF_RUN_WFM_INTERFACE and its method PROCESS_TASK_RESULT. This method directly processes the workitem ID associated with the PO approval step, mimicking the exact user action of clicking the "Approve" button in Fiori. Alternatively, if you are looking for a standard API approach, you can trigger the SAP Gateway OData service MM_PUR_PO_APV_SRV or use the OData function import for the Workflow service (/IWPGW/TASKPROCESSING).

Are you planning to run this approval logic inside a custom ABAP report/batch job, or are you building an integration with an external third-party system?

entry.title: API Journal Entry - Post (Asynchronous) - additional Freely Defined Currencies
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I am trying to use the SOAP API service:JOURNALENTRYBULKCREATIONREQUEST_INfor FI journal entry postings. 

I would like to use it also to provide amounts for additional Freely Defined Currencies, not only 1, as it is in SAP documentation.
Business case scenario is to reclassify balances only in Freely Defined Currencies in example from 1 - 4 only - just like in manual app "Post Currency Adjustments". 

Questions:

  1. Is it possible to extend or enhance JOURNALENTRYBULKCREATIONREQUEST_IN to support additional freely defined currencies? For example by adding new section for those currencies in WSDL / generated SOAP structure?
  2. Are there newer API versions/services that expose all configured freely defined currencies?
  3. Is this limitation expected by design?
  4. Would SAP recommend using another API/BAPI instead (for example BAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST) for postings with multiple freely defined currencies?

I would appreciate any experience or recommendations from projects using multiple freely defined currencies with FI posting APIs.

Thanks!

entry.title: Re: Pacemaker workcenter requirement for capacity schedul...
entry.content:
Hi Daniel,

You've hit on a known constraint regarding how the Fiori Capacity Scheduling Board app handles order dispatching logic.

To answer your main question: no, you don't necessarily have to explode your master data into x separate production versions. If your lines are completely identical and interchangeable, the standard SAP recommendation is to use a Line Hierarchy (transaction L01). By creating a header work center to represent the entire group of lines and assigning the individual lines underneath it, you can maintain just one production version referencing that header. The scheduling board will read the capacity at the header level, and you can handle the specific line dispatching within the app.

You are also completely right about your side note. While the 'Production Line' field text historically leans toward Repetitive Manufacturing, S/4HANA utilizes this specific field in the Production Version as a generic anchor point to identify the primary Pacemaker for advanced scheduling apps, regardless of whether you are running REM or Discrete.

Are you currently working with standard Production Orders, or are your orders generated via PP/DS (Advanced Planning) where alternative modes could be utilized instead?

entry.title: Re: Requirement to Display IM Storage Bin in Production O...
entry.content:
Good day @kkumara 

Thank you for your question.

The IM storage bin value is maintained at material, plant and storage location level. Technically, this is MARD-LGPBE. For the Production Order Pick List, the supported extension point is the Pick List from data source, not a direct modification of an internal CDS view.

The business context for you to start with for a component level Pick List field is Reservation Document Item, because production order components are handled at reservation item level for picking and goods issue. See KBA 3484577 for more details.

This is my next steps recommendation:

  1. Confirm the raw material storage bin is maintained in the product master for the correct plant and storage location.
  2. Confirm the production order component uses the same plant and storage location.
  3. Open Maintain Form Templates and identify the Pick List from data source.                                                                                                          
  4. Check whether Storage Bin is available through a data source extension.
  5. If yes, add it as a standard field and replace it in the custom Pick List form.
  6. If no, then ensure you create a custom field using Reservation Document Item, enable Form Template usage and populate it through a supported extensibility mechanism.
  7. Add the field to the custom XDP form in Adobe LiveCycle Designer.
  8. Assign the custom template and regenerate the Pick List.

 

Here are references for more guidance

3082452 - App Stock - Multiple materials does not display Storage Bin - SAP for Me

3483228 - How to add a standard field on the manufacturing order output - SAP for Me

3485035 - How to use custom form template for manufacturing order output - SAP for Me

3550037 - Typical issues and questions regarding production order output in S/4HANA Cloud public edition. - SAP for Me

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Adding Immutable Blockchain Proofs to SAP With CAP - Cardano and OData V4
entry.content:
ODATANO: Turning Cardano into a CAP Plugin

Hi everyone. I'm Max, a SAP developer for 10 years. In my spare time, I've been experimenting with Blockchain and immutable data proofs a lot lately. In particular, with Cardano, because it's one of the few public blockchains built on peer-reviewed research and formal methods, which matters a lot when you're thinking about anchoring any kind of enterprise data. So I tried to work out a way to bring the two worlds together.

This is a write-up of what came out of it. I hope this is useful. Feedback and pushbacks are very welcome.

Why is blockchain next to SAP at all

SAP systems are trusted but mutable. An admin can edit records, a migration can rewrite values, and an audit trail living in the same database as the data it tracks is only as trustworthy as the permissions around it.

A public blockchain solves that one specific problem: immutable, externally verifiable proof that a piece of data existed at a given moment, outside any one organization's trust boundary.

The use cases aren't exotic at all. Audit anchoring, batch traceability, cross-company verification, and regulatory compliance are just a few. So the hard part isn't the use case. It's the integration. Chain-specific SDKs, raw cryptographic payloads, wallet protocols, and provider APIs have nothing in common with the OData services that SAP Systems are used to consuming.

As I got more familiar with CAP, I thought to myself, what if the blockchain were just another data source behind a CAP service?

The CAP reflex

Anyone who's worked with CAP knows the regular pattern. Model the domain in CDS, write service handlers, let the framework handle OData, routing, metadata, and authentication.

Cardano's domain maps surprisingly cleanly to CDS. Blocks, Transactions, UTxOs, Assets, Stake Pools, Epochs are all typed entities with relationships. A Cardano transaction, for example, always has an input and output composition. And one Output has a composition of different Assets. An Asset has policyId, assetName, fingerprint, and quantity. Conceptually, no further from SAP than, say, an external material service with detailed classifications.

If the domain can be modeled, the rest can too.

What ODATANO is

ODATANO is a CAP application that exposes Cardano as three OData V4 services. More importantly, it's packaged as a CAP plugin (@odatano/core on npm). If you've used @cap-js/sqlite or @SAP/cds-hana,  You know the drill: install the package, add a config block, plugin self-registers at startup.

No custom middleware. No manual route wiring. No code changes in your host app.

Setup

npm install @odatano/core @cap-js/sqlite

An example of the “cds” section in a package.jsonconsumer project might look like this:

 
"cds": {
    "requires": {
      "[development]": {
        "auth": { "kind": "dummy"}
      },
      "db": {
        "kind": "sqlite",
        "credentials": { "url": "db.sqlite" }
      },
      "odatano-core": {
        "network": "preview",
        "backends": [ "koios" ],
        "txBuilders": [ "buildooor" ]
      }
    }
  }

The plugin auto-registers and is available after

cds deploy --to sqlite && cds watch

Three OData services are now live alongside your own, same CAP runtime, same auth middleware, full $metadata discovery.

The three services

Read (/odata/v4/cardano-odata) 18 entities: Blocks, Transactions, Addresses, UTxOs, Assets, Stake Pools, Epochs, Accounts, DReps, Protocol Parameters. Full OData V4 query surface: $filter, $select, $expand, $orderby, $top, $skip, $count. 

Transaction (/odata/v4/cardano-transaction) 11 actions covering the full Build/Sign/Submit lifecycle. ADA transfers, multi-asset, native token minting, CIP-20 metadata, Plutus V3. Each action returns a TransactionBuilds entity with fee estimate, unsigned CBOR, and outputs.

Signing (/odata/v4/cardano-sign) 8 actions implementing external signing as a state machine (pending → verified → submitted). CIP-30 wallets, Cardano CLI, PKCS#11 HSMs. The service never touches private keys.

Total: 31 entities, 34 actions, three services.

Why OData & typed entities matter

Every Cardano entity is a proper CDS model with associations and compositions. That changes what consumers can do:

GET /odata/v4/cardano-odata/Transactions('abc123')?$expand=inputs,outputs($expand=assets)

Response is structured and typed. Fiori, Excel, Power BI, custom consumers, nobody has to parse raw blockchain data.

Same principle for actions. Every parameter has a name, type, and annotations. API Business Hub and Gateway Client render input forms from the $metadata document alone.

CAP Architecture patterns

I tried not to invent much. Where CAP had a pattern, I used it.

Service handlers wrap business logic in handleRequest(), which catches blockchain-specific errors and maps them to OData responses. For example, 400 for validation, 404 for missing UTxOs, 503 for backend outages, so service consumers never see a raw chain exception.

Lazy on-demand indexing. No bulk replication. First query for a block, transaction, or address fetches from the backend, persists viaUPSERT in the Database, and serves subsequent requests from cache until TTL expires.

Multi-backend failover. Three backends (Blockfrost, Koios, Ogmios). The orchestrator picks the right one per request type (Ogmios for live UTxOs, Blockfrost for historical) and fails over on timeout. Transparent to the consumer.

Database-agnostic. CDS entities and standard CAP persistence. SQLite locally, HANA on BTP. No code change, no schema migration. CDS compiler handles the DDL.

TRACE: a concrete use case

To make this less abstract and to test the plugin firsthand, I built a second CAP app: TRACE, a Fiori frontend for pharmaceutical supply chain tracking. TRACE just consumes @odatano/core as a plugin and can use all of the available services for interacting with the Cardano blockchain without having to write one line of custom blockchain code.

Business Scenario: A manufacturer produces a batch. Regulation requires tamper-proof evidence of every custody transfer from the manufacturer to the distributor to the pharmacy. Nobody can rewrite history afterward.

The manufacturer mints the batch as an NFT via ODATANOs BuildMintTransaction OData Action. The NFT carries an inline datum (manufacturer ID, batch ID, custody state) written on-chain at creation.

Each Batch handover calls the OData Action BuildPlutusSpendTransaction. The Plutus V3 Cardano Smartcontract validator enforces on-chain who's allowed to transfer. inlineDatumJson carries the updated custody state to the continuing output.

Any party, manufacturer, regulator, pharmacy, or end consumer can read the full custody chain without access to anyone's SAP system. Because the data lives on Cardano, not in any vendor database.

Conclusion

ODATANO started as a personal question: could a public Blockchain like Cardano, with smart contracts & transaction logic, feel like another OData Service? After half a year of building, I think the answer is yes. Three OData services, familiar CAP patterns, and TRACE as proof that a real consumer app doesn't need a single line of blockchain-specific code.  What excites me most is that this way, Cardano just shows up as another service in the stack, with the same $metadata, the same query syntax, the same handlers as any other CAP service, just with tamper-proof storage and cryptographic guarantees by design.

Status

Funded by Cardano Catalyst Fund 14 (Open Developers). Apache 2.0. Final milestone approved late April, no reviewer refusals, about two months before the deadline.

  • Version: v1.7.8 (@odatano/core on npm)
  • Tests: 1,285 tests across 31 suites, 97% statement coverage
  • Cardano Networks: mainnet, preview, preprod
  • Backends: Blockfrost, Koios, Ogmios (with a real Cardano node)
  • CAP compatibility: @SAP/cds 9.x, Node.js 18+
  • Main Repo: github.com/ODATANO/ODATANO
  • TRACE (Example Consumer App):  TRACE 
  • Homepage: https://odatano.dev/ 

If anyone wants to poke at it, try it, tear it apart, please do. I'm especially interested in feedback from people deep in CAP, because I'm never quite sure which patterns are the most idiomatic. Issues, threads, and messages are all welcome.

Thanks and best regards

Max

entry.title: Restructured Account Category Management: Complete Day 1 Impact Overview
entry.content:
SAP Fieldglass is introducing a restructured Account Category Management framework to standardize how account categories are derived, validated, and consumed across Statements of Work, Events, Cost Centers, and integrations. This enhancement improves data consistency and aligns with SAP standards, and ensures that existing processes continue without disruption on Day 1.

This blog provides a complete view of Day 1 behavior, system impacts, and required customer actions.

Scope and Applicability

This enhancement applies only to customers who have enabled the Account Category pick list and are using Event-based SOWs. Within this scope, the system introduces consistent logic for deriving and enforcing account categories across SOW headers, Events, and Cost Center assignments.

Ensuring Continuity with None (NA)

To prevent disruption during transition, the system introduces None (NA), a system-driven default account category used when a valid value cannot be derived.

On Day 1, if cost centers have different account categories, the system automatically assigns None (NA) at the SOW header or event level. This value is visible in the user interface and remains locked during revisions, ensuring consistency once applied.

The None (NA) option is enabled by default, where required for conversion and can be controlled through the Account Category pick list. Customers can disable it later; however, doing so affects only newly created SOWs. This approach allows organizations to stabilize their data and integrations before enforcing stricter validation.

Day 1 Behavior Across SOW and Event Scenarios

System behavior on Day 1 depends on whether associated cost centers share the same account category.

When all cost centers have a common account category, the system automatically derives and populates that value at the SOW header or Event level. This value is displayed in SOW details, revision forms, and Event pages, and remains locked during revisions. At the Event level, account category is locked when there is active spend on the SOW.

When cost centers have different account categories, the system cannot derive a single valid value and instead assigns None (NA). This applies at both the SOW and Event levels and remains locked during revisions under the same conditions.

From a supplier perspective, this introduces controlled flexibility. When a valid account category is derived, suppliers can only select cost centers aligned with that account category during cost allocation. When None (NA) is applied, suppliers can initially select any cost center. However, if they update the account category to a valid value, the system immediately enforces alignment and restricts cost center selection accordingly.

Special Handling of “U” (Unknown Account Category)

The handling of Account Category U changes significantly and has immediate Day 1 impact.

At the admin cost center level, the account category drop down no longer displays U as an option. Instead, once Account Category U is enabled in the pick list, the system automatically creates a system-generated cost center.

For buyers, if existing SOWs or Events use cost centers with account category U, the cost center section is no longer displayed and is no longer mandatory. The system automatically assigns the backend- generated cost center U with 100% allocation.

For suppliers, the system enforces stricter validation. Line items cannot be submitted if they are tied to cost centers with Account category U. Suppliers must select a valid cost center with a defined account category before submission. This applies consistently across standard line items, fees, and schedule-based submissions, including hierarchical events.

This validation is enforced by default and is no longer dependent on configuration or rules. For fee and schedule submissions, suppliers and workers are required to select chargeable cost centers.

Integration Behavior and Validation Logic

 Account Category Derivation

If the Account Category is not provided at the Event level or SOW Header level for connectors such as SOW Shell Upload, Event Hierarchy Upload, SOW XML, or SOWR XML, the system derives the account category value from the associated cost centres.

Same Account Category Across Cost Centres

When all associated cost centres belong to the same Account Category, the Event or SOW Account Category is set to that value.

Mixed Account Category and Blank Values

When the Cost Centres are associated with or without account category, the system refers to the Account Category Admin pick list configuration and defaults the Event or SOW Account Category to None (NA).

Multiple Account Categories

When the associated Cost Centres belong to multiple Account Categories, the system refers to the Account Category Admin pick list configuration and sets the Event or SOW Account Category to None (NA ).

SOW XML / SOWR XML Processing

If no Account Category is provided at the header level, the Header Account Category defaults to None (NA).

Mixed or Blank Account Categories at Header Level

If Cost Centres contain multiple Account Categories, the Header Account Category defaults to None (NA). 

Changes to Configuration, Templates, and Admin Setup

In addition to the core behavior, several supporting changes take effect on Day 1 to simplify configuration and enforce consistency.

Account Category management is now centralized through the Account Category pick list admin object, where both standard and custom account categories can be maintained. Cost centers that do not align with predefined standard account categories are treated as custom and can be configured accordingly within this framework.

Several legacy configurations are removed or streamlined.

  • Account category validation for Events is no longer available.
  • The dependency on the Allow Unknown Cost Center Validation setting is removed for SOW-related processes, including schedules, events, hierarchical events, fees, revised line item holdbacks, and milestone SOWs. However, this configuration continues to apply to Services and Contingent workflows, such as time sheets, expense sheets, and miscellaneous invoices.
  • Rules that previously allowed cost allocation adjustments when unknown cost centers were present are removed from both SOW templates and the SOW details page.

 These changes re-inforce a shift toward stricter, more standardized validation, and eliminate exception-based handling.

What Customers Need to Do Next

While Day 1 ensures continuity through fallback mechanisms, customers are expected to transition towards full adoption of the new model.

Organizations should begin by aligning their Account Category pick list with ERP configurations, ensuring that all required account categories are available. Existing cost objects should be reviewed and updated to ensure the Account Category field is correctly populated.

Once master data and integrations are fully aligned, customers can disable None (NA) from the Account Category pick list, completing the transition to a fully governed and standardized model.

Conclusion

The restructured Account Category Management framework introduces a more consistent, controlled, and scalable approach to managing account categories across SOW workflows. Day 1 changes are designed to maintain business continuity while guiding customers toward improved data quality and system alignment.

By proactively updating master data and integrations, organizations can quickly move beyond transitional defaults and fully realize the benefits of this enhanced framework.

You can also have a look at the What’s New Viewer for SAP Fieldglass for more details:

entry.title: Re: Custom notifications and widget events in SAP DM POD 2.0
entry.content:
Hi @JensVG ,

You can create a PPD with ‘POD Connection’ for POD 2.0 enabled:

and use ‘Notification Send’ service to push a notification to PODs, like:

Of course, you can make all input parameters dynamic, and trigger this PPD from anywhere, like an Automatic Trigger / Subscription, or something else.

Then, you can build a custom POD 2.0 Plugin or Action to intercept this notification, you can find an example here: https://github.com/SAP-samples/digital-manufacturing-extension-samples/blob/main/dm-podplugin-extensions/custom-pod2-examples/actions/ServerNotificationHandlerAction.js

More details:

Implement POD Notifications

You can implement custom POD notifications for the widgets that you develop.

To support automation scenarios, PODs support POD notifications. A POD notification is triggered from an outside entity, such as a machine, and it's delivered to one or more PODs.

Since the type sap.dm.dme.pod2.notification.EventType is defined on both the server and the user interface, you can implement custom POD notifications for the widgets that you develop and supported events by implementing the following sample code and adjusting it according to your needs:

Sample Code
 
const oSubscriptionContext = PodNotificationWebSocket.subscribe({
             eventType: EventType.CUSTOM,
             onMessage: (message) => {
                 console.log("Received message.eventType: ", message.eventType);
                 console.log("Received message.topic: ", message.topic);
                 console.log("Message value: ", message.data.parameters[0].value);
             },
             filter: Filter.and([
                   Filter.equals("subscription.plant", "123"),
                   Filter.equals("eventName", "MEASUREMENT")
             ]),
             description: "MyCustomHandler"
         });
In the sample code, the filter says that the server should forward any message that has both plant 123 and eventName "MEASUREMENT" to the POD or plugin that's executing this function.
Therefore, when a custom message is published on /notification/v1/send, it has the following payload:

Sample Code
 
{
    "eventName" : "MEASUREMENT",
    "subscription": {
        "plant": "123",
        "resource", "MIXING"
    },
    "parameters": [ {
        "name": "message",
        "value": "This is a custom notification message"
    } ]
}
 
When the message is delivered, the console displays the following:

Sample Code
 
Received message.eventType: sap.dsc.dm.GENERIC.MESSAGE.v1
Received message.topic: production
Received value: This is a custom notification message.

You can also explore the github examples above, for more details.

Hope it helps! Br, Manoel

entry.title: Re: SAP Analytics Cloud - You Want to Export to Excel? Not Today. 🚫📊
entry.content:
@Tony_H2 
I absolutely agree! Excel isn't going away, nor should it. When users are pulling data from S/4, SAC, Snowflake and more into a single workbook, Excel is a pragmatic solution. The goal shouldn't be to eliminate that entirely, but to make sure it happens in a governed and intentional way.

That said, the preferred approach should still be to provide all relevant data centrally (e.g. via Datasphere and SAC) – ideally through a single, governed semantic layer that users can trust and rely on. If users feel the need to stitch together data from multiple sources in Excel, that's often a signal that the central data offering isn't meeting their needs yet. Thanks for raising that perspective!

entry.title: Re: INSERT errors out
entry.content:
What was tempdb's setting for 'allow nulls by default' at the time the table was created?  Had you set 'allow nulls by default' to 'false' at some time in the past?

Running sp_dboption tempdb, 'allow nulls by default', 'true'  will affect future column creations; it will not modify columns that have already been created. 

What does sp_help abcatedt show as the NULLability for column abe_edit?

After running sp_dboption tempdb, 'allow nulls by default', 'true' , if you drop and recreate the table, does your code work (ie, the column will now accept NULLs)?

entry.title: Re: SAP Analytics Cloud - You Want to Export to Excel? Not Today. 🚫📊
entry.content:
@Robert_Shepherd1 

Great point and a very valid concern! You're right – disabling the Data Analyzer globally is a blunt instrument, and given how central it is to SAC reporting and Insights, it's not a realistic option for most organisations.

To be transparent: there is no granular export control within the Data Analyzer itself. Once a user has access to it, the export options (Excel, CSV, PDF) are available. The only current UI-level lever is restricting access to the Data Analyzer entirely – which, as you rightly note, is often not desirable.

However, there is a more targeted approach on the data layer: through Datasphere permissions you can control which analytical models a user has access to in the first place. This means you can effectively limit which models are available in the Data Analyzer on a per-user or per-role basis – without disabling the tool entirely. It's not a direct export restriction, but it does allow for a more granular governance approach upstream.

Whether SAP has more fine-grained Data Analyzer export controls on the roadmap I honestly don't know – but it might be worth raising a request through the SAP Influence portal!

entry.title: Re: Automatic Purchase order generate during MIGO posting

entry.title: How to effectively configure the "Employment Differentiator" to distinguish multiple employments?
entry.content:
Hello,

I have a question regarding the best way to distinguish multiple employment records. 

In our SuccessFactors Employee Central and Performance & Goals environment, we have users with Concurrent Employments, and it's becoming difficult for managers to tell which record they are selecting in the Employment Switcher. 

  1. Currently, we are using Company, Department, and a custom field for Expatriate/Concurrent status.
    However, we’ve encountered a challenge: some employment records are created solely for workflow/approval purposes. This leads to cases where a single employee has multiple records with identical Company, Department, and Expatriate/Concurrent status (as shown in the attached image).

  2. We have considered creating a custom field to manually flag the purpose of each employment record (e.g., "For Approval Only"). However, this would significantly increase the administrative workload for our HR team. Furthermore, we are concerned that manual entries might not be intuitive enough for users to understand at a glance.

    Any advice, screenshots, or links to implementation guides would be greatly appreciated!
    Thanks in advance for your help.

entry.title: Part 3 - Workflow n8n Integration with Joule Studio
entry.content:
SAP Community — Technology Blogs n8n meets SAP — 3-Part Series
Part 3 of 3

Workflow n8n Integration with Joule Studio

Series Overview

  1. Enterprise Automation — Deploying n8n on SAP BTP Kyma
  2. Create a Custom Language Model Node for SAP AI Core
  3. Workflow n8n Integration with Joule Studio 📍

In the first two parts of this series we deployed n8n on SAP BTP, Kyma runtime and built a custom node that connects n8n's AI Agent to SAP AI Core's Generative AI Hub. In Part 2 we also built a concrete workflow, the IT Helpdesk Assistant, an agent that can query tickets, knowledge articles, and employee skills from a live OData API and answer questions in natural language.

In this final part, we take that workflow one step further. Instead of accessing it only through n8n's own chat interface, we connect it to Joule Studio so that a Joule agent can discover and call the n8n workflow tools directly. The bridge between the two systems is the Model Context Protocol (MCP), an open standard for exposing tools to AI agents in a structured, discoverable way.

1 Publish the Workflow in n8n

Before anything else, the IT Helpdesk Agent workflow from Part 2 needs to be active and running on your Kyma n8n instance. Open your live n8n instance at your Kyma domain URL, navigate to the workflow and click the Publish Button in the top-right corner of the editor.

An published workflow means its trigger is live and listening. Without the workflow being published, it will not appear in the MCP Server's tool list.

 

 

2 Enable the MCP Server in n8n

n8n has a built-in MCP Server feature that exposes all active workflows with a Chat Trigger as discoverable tools — following the Model Context Protocol standard. Any MCP-compatible client, including Joule Studio, can connect to this endpoint and see exactly which tools are available and how to call them.

To enable it, go to Settings in your n8n instance and navigate to the MCP Server section. Enable the feature. n8n will generate two pieces of information that you will need in the next steps:

MCP Server URLThe base URL of your n8n instance — the same Kyma domain URL from Part 1, for example https://n8n.<your-kyma-domain>
Access TokenA bearer token generated by n8n that MCP clients must present with every request to authenticate themselves

Copy both values and keep them at hand — you will use them when creating the BTP Destination in the next step.

 

Treat the MCP access token like any other credential. Anyone who holds this token can call your n8n workflows remotely. Do not share it, do not commit it to a repository, and rotate it if you suspect it has been exposed.
 
 
The n8n Settings page with the MCP Server section open — showing the feature enabled and the generated Access Token and URL.

 

 

3 Create the BTP Destination

Joule Studio does not call external services directly. It goes through the SAP BTP Destination Service — a managed proxy layer that handles connectivity configurations centrally. To connect Joule to n8n, we register the n8n MCP endpoint as a destination in our BTP subaccount and add a set of properties that tell Joule Studio this destination is an MCP server.

Open the Destination Configuration

In the SAP BTP Cockpit, navigate to your subaccount and open Connectivity → Destinations. Create New Destination.

Basic Configuration

Fill in the destination properties as follows:

NameA descriptive name you will recognise in Joule Studio, for example n8n-helpdesk-mcp
TypeHTTP
URLThe base URL of your n8n Kyma instance — for example https://n8n.<your-kyma-domain>
AuthenticationNo Authentication — the token is passed separately via an additional property below

Additional Properties

Below the basic fields, there is an Additional Properties section. This is where the MCP-specific configuration lives. Add the following two entries:

Key Value
sap-joule-studio-mcp-servertrue
URL.headers.AuthorizationThe access token from n8n — paste the full token value here

The first property flags this destination to Joule Studio as an MCP server — without it, the destination will not appear in the MCP server selector inside Joule Studio. The second property injects the n8n access token as an Authorization header on every outbound request, which is how n8n authenticates incoming MCP calls.

Click Save.

 


The completed BTP Destination form — showing the Name, URL, Authentication  and both Additional Properties

 

4 Create the Agent in Joule Studio

With the destination in place, open Joule Studio from your BTP Cockpit subscriptions. Navigate to the Agents section and create a new agent.

Agent Identity

Give the agent a clear name and fill in the description fields:

NameFor example IT Helpdesk Assistant
DescriptionA short explanation of what the agent does — this is displayed to end users. For example: "Assists with IT support requests by looking up tickets, knowledge articles, and employee skills."
ExpertiseRole or Persona for the Agent eg. "IT Helpdesk assistant"
InstructionsA more detailed prompt that guides the agent's reasoning. Describe the domain it operates in and any behavioral expectations.

5 Connect the MCP Server

Below the model settings section, Joule Studio provides an option to add an MCP Server to the agent. This is where you link the BTP Destination you created in the previous step.

 

1. Select the MCP Server

Click Add MCP Server. A selector will appear listing all BTP Destinations in your subaccount that have the sap-joule-studio-mcp-server: true property set. Select the destination you created — for example n8n-helpdesk-mcp.

 

2. Set the Path in Advanced Options

Expand the Advanced Options section for the MCP server entry. Set the Path field to:

/mcp-server/http/

This path is the MCP endpoint that n8n exposes on its server. It tells Joule Studio exactly where within the destination URL to send MCP discovery and tool call requests.

 

Joule Studio — the MCP Server section with the destination selected and the Advanced Options expanded

 

3. Verify the Tool List

After saving the MCP server configuration, Joule Studio will attempt to connect to the n8n MCP endpoint and retrieve the list of available tools. If the destination, token, and path are all correct, a list will appear directly in the interface showing every active n8n tools.

 

Seeing the tool list appear is the confirmation that the full integration is working end-to-end. Joule Studio has successfully discovered the n8n MCP server, authenticated using the token from the destination, and retrieved the tool schema. No further wiring is needed — the agent can now call these tools in a live conversation.
 
Click Apply.

Joule Studio — the tool list populated after a successful MCP connection

 

 

What to Check if the Tool List Does Not Appear

If the list remains empty or an error is shown, work through the following in order:

  • Confirm them MCP Server is enabled in n8n
  • Check that the URL in the BTP Destination points to the correct Kyma domain and does not have a trailing slash conflict with the path.
  • Verify the access token in URL.headers.Authorization is current — n8n may rotate or invalidate tokens when the MCP Server is reconfigured.
  • Ensure the path in Joule Studio's Advanced Options is exactly /mcp-server/http/ including the trailing slash.

6 Test the Agent

Save the agent configuration and open the built-in test inside Joule Studio. Type a question that maps to one of the tool's capabilities — for example:  "What are the currently open tickets?" 

Joule will process the message, identify the appropriate tool, send an MCP tool call through the BTP Destination to n8n, and receive the OData response. The final answer presented in the chat will be a summary of the live data now surfaced through Joule's conversational interface.

 
Joule Studio test chat with the Agent reasoning

 

 

 

✓ Series Complete

Over three posts we built a complete, production-oriented integration stack connecting n8n and the SAP ecosystem — from infrastructure to AI to conversational interfaces.


Part 1 Deployed n8n self-hosted on SAP BTP Kyma runtime with persistent storage and an external API Rule
Part 2 Built a custom n8n node bridging SAP AI Core's Generative AI Hub and deployed an IT Helpdesk Agent workflow
Part 3 Exposed the n8n workflow as an MCP server, connected it to Joule Studio via a BTP Destination and gave a Joule agent access 
 
 
 

AI Usage Disclosure: Gen AI was used exclusively for linguistic refinements such as grammar, spelling, and phrasing as well as for the structural organisation of this text. All conceptual content, technical knowledge, architecture decisions, and implementation steps were developed independently by the author.

 
SAP Community — Technology Blogs n8n meets SAP — Part 3 of 3

entry.title: Bank Statement Date in Closed Period
entry.content:
Hi Dear SAP Support,

We are facing an issue related to the processing of bank statements and would appreciate your support in resolving it.

The uploaded bank statement has a statement date of 11.12.2025. When attempting to reprocess the bank statement items, the system generates an error message indicating that the posting period is closed.

As reopening an already closed period is not a preferred option, we would like to process and post the bank statement using the currently open posting period, which is May 2026.

Could you please advise on how we can avoid these error messages and proceed with posting the bank statement in the current open period?

entry.title: Why ERP still defines the limits of AI in the enterprise
entry.content:
Introduction

There’s been a lot of noise around AI and how much it’s changing enterprise systems. It is undeniable that changes are here to stay. We’ve seen teams getting better forecasts, automating things that used to take forever, and catching issues earlier than before. Even we as an enterprise have evolved into something that I have no idea how it would function if AI suddenly disappeared one day.

But after a while, the conversation shifts. You can do countless of things with AI, and I think that is what everyone else is focusing on. And what else? What comes after checking whether AI works or not? What do you actually do once it's there? Getting recommendations is not the hard part anymore. You can have a model suggesting changes, flagging risks, or pointing at opportunities, and most of the time it makes sense. The problem shows up later, when you try to use that in real processes. That’s usually where things start to slow down, and where ERP suddenly matters again.

The Gap Between AI Insights and Execution

We’ve seen this happen more than once. Companies expect faster decisions because they have better insights, but once those decisions need to move through finance, operations, or logistics, everything gets more complicated. Systems don’t always adapt quickly, processes depend on each other, and what looked like a simple adjustment ends up taking way more effort than expected.

In many scenarios, the real challenge emerges when you have to close the execution gap, and not when you have to add more intelligence, as many people may think. It’s something that comes up all the time in conversations with our clients: the need is there, the intent is clear, but the underlying ERP landscape just isn’t ready to move at the same speed. Closing that gap between expectation and reality is where most of the real work is happening today.

The struggle that most organizations have has todo with what comes after they have produce insights. How do you execute a decision through a system that is so structured and interconnected? Is thanks to SAP S/4HANA —and similar ERP platforms— that decision has the opportunity to become reality.

On paper, the issue is simple. AI generates an insight, the business evaluates it, and then we, as organization, execute it. The thing is that, in reality, this chain encounters a vast amount of problems that lead to decisions getting delayed or partially implemented.

In one of the projects we worked on, this became very clear. The company, a global manufacturer in the energy space, was still relying on manual processes to count and track critical components. It wasn’t just slow, it also introduced errors and even safety concerns for the people involved.

By introducing AI-based image recognition, they were able to automate this process and significantly improve both productivity and safety. However, what became clear during the implementation is that the real value didn’t come from the AI model itself, but from how that information was integrated and executed across the operation. The data captured in the field needed to be synchronized with ERP systems, trigger updates in inventory, and align with warehouse and logistics processes in real time. Without that connection, the insight would have remained isolated. It was the ability to translate that intelligence into structured actions within the system that ultimately made the difference.

In another case, a client was using predictive models to anticipate demand changes and adjust procurement accordingly. The insights were accurate, but execution lagged behind. Translating those recommendations into actual purchase orders required multiple validations and manual interventions within the ERP system. By the time the adjustments were fully implemented, the conditions had already shifted. Once again, the limitation was not the quality of the insight, but the ability to execute it in time.

This is why the conversation around ERP is becoming relevant again, especially in the context of AI. It’s no longer just about having a system to run transactions, but about having a system that can absorb change and execute decisions without friction. Concepts like clean core, standardized processes, and flexible extensions are not just technical preferences anymore. They directly impact how fast and how effectively a company can act on what AI is telling them.

Conclusion

What we are seeing, then, is a shift in how these technologies relate to each other. AI is expanding what companies can see and understand about their business, giving them more options and better insights. ERP, on the other hand, continues to define what they can actually do with that information. The gap between those two layers is where many organizations are currently stuck.

The truth is that the companies that will get the most value out of AI are the ones that connect with the models to execution, and not necessarily the ones with the most advanced models. That means treating ERP not as a static backend system, but as a dynamic platform that enables change across the organization. AI might be redefining how companies think, but ERP still defines how they act. In the enterprise, action is where value is created.

entry.title: Re: Update Loading weight in physical stock to align with...

Hi,

Material master changes do not trigger any update to existing stock lines.
Hence, SAP advises such customizing should not be changed while products exist in the warehouse.

In this scenario HU/stock weight will only be updated when you move it.

entry.title: Check out the Expert Guided Implementation (EGI) sessions for June 2026
entry.content:
Check out the Expert Guided Implementation (EGI’s) sessions for June 2026.  EGI’s are instructor led sessions.  The sessions below are designed to facilitate effective adoption and utilization of the SAP Signavio solutions.  EGIs are part of your SAP Enterprise Support or SAP Enterprise Support, Cloud Edition contract, which means there’s no additional cost for participating in these sessions.

Important:  The links in this blog connect to the SAP Learning Hub, edition for SAP Enterprise Support, which requires a one-time registration. 

📌  Sign up | Walkthrough video 

June 2026 sessions are outlined in this blog with brief descriptions. 

                                                     ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

✔️SAP Signavio Adoption Starter:  Plug and Gain approach – process discovery and analysis [English].  Register Here.  This offering provides participants with a practical, streamlines methodology for understanding and analyzing SAP ERP processes, leveraging SAP Signavio Process Insights, Process Intelligence and the Collaboration Hub.

Goals:

  • Discover and articulate the current as-is state of your business processes
  • Interpret key insights from the existing process landscape to support informed transformation decisions

                                                     ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

✔️SAP Signavio Adoption Starter:  Value Accelerators and Process Navigator [English].  Register Here.  This course helps you to adopt and consume the capability and usability of the SAP Signavio Process Explorer to enable business users to run in-depth process analysis. Also get an insight on country- and deployment-specific SAP solution value accelerators using Process Navigator, different deployment options, countries, and localization requirements.

Goals:  

  • Get SAP Signavio Process Explorer is set up and ready to use.
  • Understand how Value Accelerators can speed up your transformation journey.
  • Understand SAP Signavio Process Navigator.
  • Understand how to Navigate through different value accelerators​​.

                                                   ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

✔️ SAP Signavio Adoption Starter:  Process Insights for Business – Sales [Portuguese and Spanish].  Register Here.  This offering helps you to adopt and consume the capability and usability of the SAP Signavio Process Insights to enable business users to run in-depth process analysis. Learn How to analyze your Lead-to-Cash process with SAP Signavio Process Insights.

Goals: 

  • Analyze Lead-to-Cash process data for better decision-making.
  • Implement recommendations to optimize sales workflows.
  • Prepare for S/4HANA migration with data-driven insights.

                                       ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

✔️SAP Signavio Adoption Starter:  Process Manager and Collaboration Hub [Spanish, and Portuguese]. Register Here.  This course is designed to facilitate the effective adoption and utilization of SAP Signavio Process Manager and Collaboration Hub for process management and modeling.

 Goals: 

  • Learn about SAP Signavio and how you can initiate your organizations transformation journey with SAP Signavio Process Manager and Collaboration Hub.
  • Gain an understanding of SAP Signavio Process Manager and Collaboration Hub, including its usage, navigation, and setting up initial configurations.
  • Acquire the skills to create process models in SAP Signavio Process Manager using BPMN 2.0.
  • Familiarize yourself with other crucial SAP Signavio Solutions integral to your transformation efforts.

                                       ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

✔️SAP Signavio Adoption Starter:  Process Insights for Business – Plant Maintenance [English].  Register Here.  Learn how to analyze your Acquire to Decommission process with SAP Signavio Process Insights.

Goals:  

  • Access and interpret Acquire-to-Decommission process data.   
  • Apply recommendations to improve maintenance efficiency. 
  • Prepare for S/4HANA migration using actionable data insights.

                                            ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

✔️SAP Signavio Adoption Starter:  Process Insights for Business – Finance [English].  Register Here.  This offering helps you to adopt and consume the capability and usability of the SAP Signavio Process Insights to enable business users to run in-depth process analysis. This session focuses on processes in Finance. 

Goals:

entry.title: Closing Journal Entries Are Missing in Generated e-Ledger TXT File – SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Editi
entry.content:
Hello Community,

We are experiencing an issue in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition during the e-Ledger generation process.

When we generate the e-Ledger file via the “Run Statutory Reports” application, the closing journal entries are not included in the generated TXT file.

We have already maintained the year-end closing document type as “ZK” in the related configuration areas. However, the closing entries are still missing in the output file.

Steps to reproduce:

  1. Open the “Run Statutory Reports” application.
  2. Select the related reporting period (December 2025).
  3. Execute the e-Ledger generation process.
  4. Download the generated TXT file.
  5. Check the file content.

Actual result:
The closing journal entries are missing in the TXT output.

Expected result:
The closing journal entries should be included in the generated e-Ledger TXT file.

This is a productive system issue and critical for us, since the e-Ledger must be submitted to GİB within the legal deadline.

Has anyone experienced a similar issue or found a solution for this scenario?

Thank you.

Melissa.

entry.title: Re: Call transaction CS02 ignores AEN (AENNR) field
entry.content:
In your case, the difference is not a bug but rather how SAP transactions consume parameter IDs. CA02 (routing change) does read the parameter ID AEN (change number) when you call it with CALL TRANSACTION ... AND SKIP FIRST SCREEN. CS02 (BOM change), however, does not pick up AEN from the parameter ID in the same way. It expects the change number to be passed explicitly on the initial screen or via the transaction variant. That is why your SET PARAMETER ID 'AEN' works for CA02 but appears ignored in CS02.

To fix this, use parameter ID ECN or a BDC call to pass the change number. That will clear the warning and open CS02 with the correct change number.

entry.title: Re: SAP Analytics Cloud - You Want to Export to Excel? Not Today. 🚫📊
entry.content:
@JefB 
With that I mean the approach I described in Level 4

  • Remove Space Files permissions from consumer roles in Datasphere by creating a custom Role. This prevents users from actively browsing and selecting models in the SAC Excel Add In or the Data Analyzer in SAC, effectively disabling self-service Excel analysis — while still allowing them to consume SAC stories normally.

With that you can prevent Users to open the Analytic Models for certain spaces within the Data Analyzer. So that means you cannot give permissions for every single analytic model but for entire datasphere spaces via Scoped Roles which don't have the Space Files Permission as described in my screenshots 🙂

entry.title: Re: Business Objects Enterprise 4.3 update from SP04 patc...
entry.content: Hi Shiva, SAP support advised me to copy all the XML files in the "<INST_DIR>\setup\packagestemp" folder to the "<INST_DIR>\SAP BusinessObjects Enterprise XI 4.0\packages" folder and restart the SIA. After the restart, all the XML files disappeared from the "packages" folder. This, combined with the fact that the system tests were all positive, suggests that the upgrade was completed successfully. We hope there are no future problems. Thank you very much.
